This is turn 55. Turns 1 through 54 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 55.

# FrontierMath — Presentation of the Absolute Galois Group of Q₂

## Problem

Give an explicit profinite presentation of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/
\mathbb Q_2)$, the absolute Galois group of the 2-adic numbers. The precise
specification — the four generators $\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1$, the tame relation
$\tau^{\sigma}=\tau^2$, the 2-core condition on $x_0,x_1$, the modulus
$M = 85667662080$, the verifier against 5402 finite groups, the submission
format, and the worked $p=3$ example — is in `problem_statement.md`. Read it
first.

This is an open problem: the answer is known for every odd prime $p$ (NSW
Theorem 7.5.14) but not for $p=2$, where the odd-$p$ formula breaks because it
needs $(p+1)/2 = 3/2 \notin \widehat{\mathbb Z}$.

## Tools

You have access to:
- **code_interpreter**: a sandboxed Python 3.13 environment (numpy, sympy,
  networkx, ...), no internet, up to 64 GB RAM. Use it heavily to test candidate
  relations against small finite groups: for a group $G$, compute
  $|\mathrm{Sur}(\text{presentation}\to G)|/|\mathrm{Aut}(G)|$ and compare it to
  the number of Galois extensions of $\mathbb Q_2$ with group $G$.
- **web_search**: search for the state of the art (Jannsen–Wingberg, NSW,
  Demushkin groups, local Galois theory, David Roe's `padicIGP`).
- **File attachments**: read attached files.

## Freedom

This is an open problem. No known solution exists. You have full freedom to
explore any approach — novel reformulations, connections to other areas of
mathematics, and any theoretical framework you see fit. If an approach requires
developing new theory, pursue it.

**The most preferred outcome is a single explicit presentation that passes the
full verifier (all 5402 finite groups).** Verify candidates computationally as
you go — do not trust group-theoretic identities you have only checked on paper.
Prefer an imperfect reach for unfamiliar machinery over another confident lemma
in familiar territory.

## Protocol

End every response with:

**ESTABLISHED**: what you proved or computed this turn (may be empty). Leave
blank if you spent the turn learning new machinery — do not pad with
comfort-zone lemmas.
**GAP**: what remains.
**NEXT**: your plan for the next turn.

Tag: `[CONTINUE]`, `[BREAKTHROUGH]`, `[STUCK]`, or `[DONE]`.

## Success Criterion

An explicit presentation of $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/\mathbb Q_2)$ in
the required format whose surjection-counts match the ground truth for all
finite quotients (the full 5402-group verifier), or a rigorous proof that
resolves the problem.

Make sure to include  in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.
# FrontierMath Open Problem — A Presentation for the Absolute Galois Group of Q₂

Source: https://epoch.ai/frontiermath/open-problems/q2-absolute-galois
Contributor: David Roe (MIT). Status: OPEN.

## Problem

Give an explicit presentation, as a profinite group, of the absolute Galois
group $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/\mathbb Q_2)$ of the field
$\mathbb Q_2$ of 2-adic numbers.

For every odd prime $p$ the group $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_p/\mathbb Q_p)$
has a known explicit presentation (Jannsen–Wingberg; Neukirch–Schmidt–Wingberg
[NSW], *Cohomology of Number Fields*, Theorem 7.5.14). For $p=2$ no such
presentation is known — the odd-$p$ formula breaks because it involves
$(p+1)/2 = 3/2 \notin \widehat{\mathbb Z}$. Supplying a presentation for $p=2$
is the open problem.

## Form of a solution

A solution is a finite presentation with four distinguished generators
$\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1$ subject to:

- an implicit tame relation $\tau^{\sigma}=\tau^{2}$ (assumed; not part of the
  submission);
- $x_0,x_1$ lie in the 2-core $O_2(G)$ (the intersection of all Sylow-2
  subgroups) of every finite quotient $G$;
- one or more explicit "wild" relations (the content of the submission);
- exponents lie in $\widehat{\mathbb Z}$ and are reduced modulo
  $M = 85667662080 = 2^8\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 11\cdot 13\cdot 17\cdot 19\cdot 23$.

Two useful idempotents of $\widehat{\mathbb Z}/M\widehat{\mathbb Z}$:
$\omega_2 = 40491355905$ ($\equiv 1 \bmod 256$, $\equiv 0$ on the odd part) and
$\omega_{\mathrm{odd}} = 45176306176$ ($\equiv 0 \bmod 256$, $\equiv 1$ on the
odd part). Note $\omega_2 \equiv 0 \bmod 3$ and $\bmod 5$.

## Verification (what "correct" means)

The problem author has a verifier that tests a candidate presentation against
**5402 finite groups**. For each group $G$ it computes
$|\mathrm{Sur}(\text{presentation} \to G)| / |\mathrm{Aut}(G)|$ (surjections up
to automorphism) and checks it equals the ground-truth number of Galois
extensions of $\mathbb Q_2$ with Galois group $G$. A submission is correct iff
all 5402 counts match.

## Submission format

```
sigma,tau,x0,x1

[optional definitions: name = expression]

[relations: expressions that must equal the identity]
```

## Worked example for p = 3 (given; the analogue for p = 2 is what is sought)

```
sigma,tau,x0,x1

tau2 = tau^3011753745
sigma2 = sigma^3011753745
rho = tau2^4
angle = (x0*tau*x0^-1*tau)^2974571600
u = (x1*(rho^2)*x1*(rho^2))^2974571600
v = sigma2*tau2
w = (u*(v^2)*u^-1*(v^2))^2974571600
y1 = x1^rho*(u^v)*(w^(sigma2*tau2^2))*(w^(tau2^2))
comm = x1^-1*y1^-1*x1*y1

(x0^sigma)^-1*angle*x1^3*comm
```

This realizes NSW Theorem 7.5.14 for $p=3$: $\mathtt{tau2},\mathtt{sigma2}$ are
pro-$p$ projections, $\mathtt{angle}=\langle x_0,\tau\rangle$, $\mathtt{u},
\mathtt{w}$ are nested brackets, and the relation reads
$x_0^{\sigma}=\langle x_0,\tau\rangle\, x_1^{p}\,[x_1,y_1]$.

## Author's guidance

"I don't expect major conceptual developments to be required — the answer is
likely to be messy." So the sought relation may involve many intermediate
definitions (as in the $p=3$ example), not just a single short commutator word.

## Warm-up

The presentation for $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_3/\mathbb Q_3)$ is the
example above; the task is the genuinely open $p=2$ case.

Make sure to include  in your response to cite this file, or to surface it as a link.

The absolute Galois group of Q2
Problem statement
Give a presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers as a profinite group.
Overview
The absolute Galois group Gal(K/K) of a field K is the projective limit of all of the finite Galois groups Gal(E/K).
It packages together the information about all finite extensions, and studying the Galois group of the rational field
Q is a central problem in algebraic number theory. One method for approaching this group is to study the analogous
Galois groups of p-adic fields Qp. In this case, there is an explicit presentation of Gal(Qp/Qp) for p > 2, described
in Section . For p = 2, we have a description for the absolute Galois group of some extensions of Q2, but not for Q2
itself. Finding such a description would fill in a gap in our explicit understanding of Galois groups, and would have
consequences in terms of counting p-adic fields with a given Galois group.
Background

Importance
Galois representations are at the core of many problems in modern number theory. There are many perspectives on
Galois representations, but if you have access to a presentation of an absolute Galois group then they can be viewed
very concretely. The case of p = 2 often needs to be handled separately in number theory, and global applications
often require the ability to handle all primes.
Di!culty
The last major work on finding presentations for p-adic fields was done in the 1980s. To a large extent, I believe that
the di!culty is that the answer is likely to be messy, as evidenced by the complicated relation needed in the case
that p is odd. Hopefully, this feature makes the problem a good target for solution by an AI since I don’t expect
major conceptional developments to be required.
Submitting a presentation
A submission for this problem consists of a file with the following form:
variables
definitions
relations
• Sections should be separated by single blank lines, and the definitions section is optional.
• The variables should consist of a comma separated list of variable names on one line corresponding to the
generators of the presentation. The first and second generators (say ϑ and ϖ respectively) are distinguished,
and must satisfy the implicit relation ϖε = ϖ p (which should not be included in the relations section). All
later generators should be contained within the p-core of the group (the intersection of all p-Sylow subgroups).
• In the definitions section, you may define additional variables in terms of the generators and variables
defined earlier within the definitions section. Each line should take the form var = expr, where expr is an
expression built using group operations.
• In the relations section, you should give the relations as expressions in terms of variables defined in the
previous two sections. If there are multiple relations, give one on each line.
Expressions in profinite groups may involve exponents lying in Zˆ. To specify such a constant, you should provide
its reduction modulo 85667662080 = 28 · 32 · 5 · 7 · 11 · 13 · 17 · 19 · 23, which is the least common multiple of the
exponents of all the groups being used to verify the presentation.
References
[1] J¨urgen Neukirch, Alexander Schmidt, and Kay Wingberg. Cohomology of Number Fields. 2nd ed. Berlin:
Springer-Verlag, 2015.
[2] Luis Ribes and Pavel Zalesskii. Profinite Groups. 2nd ed. Berlin: Springer-Verlag, 2010.

# FrontierMath Open Problem — A Presentation for the Absolute Galois Group of Q₂

Source: https://epoch.ai/frontiermath/open-problems/q2-absolute-galois
Contributor: David Roe (MIT). Status: OPEN.

## Problem

Give an explicit presentation, as a profinite group, of the absolute Galois
group $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_2/\mathbb Q_2)$ of the field
$\mathbb Q_2$ of 2-adic numbers.

For every odd prime $p$ the group $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_p/\mathbb Q_p)$
has a known explicit presentation (Jannsen–Wingberg; Neukirch–Schmidt–Wingberg
[NSW], *Cohomology of Number Fields*, Theorem 7.5.14). For $p=2$ no such
presentation is known — the odd-$p$ formula breaks because it involves
$(p+1)/2 = 3/2 \notin \widehat{\mathbb Z}$. Supplying a presentation for $p=2$
is the open problem.

## Form of a solution

A solution is a finite presentation with four distinguished generators
$\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1$ subject to:

- an implicit tame relation $\tau^{\sigma}=\tau^{2}$ (assumed; not part of the
  submission);
- $x_0,x_1$ lie in the 2-core $O_2(G)$ (the intersection of all Sylow-2
  subgroups) of every finite quotient $G$;
- one or more explicit "wild" relations (the content of the submission);
- exponents lie in $\widehat{\mathbb Z}$ and are reduced modulo
  $M = 85667662080 = 2^8\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 11\cdot 13\cdot 17\cdot 19\cdot 23$.

Two useful idempotents of $\widehat{\mathbb Z}/M\widehat{\mathbb Z}$:
$\omega_2 = 40491355905$ ($\equiv 1 \bmod 256$, $\equiv 0$ on the odd part) and
$\omega_{\mathrm{odd}} = 45176306176$ ($\equiv 0 \bmod 256$, $\equiv 1$ on the
odd part). Note $\omega_2 \equiv 0 \bmod 3$ and $\bmod 5$.

## Verification (what "correct" means)

The problem author has a verifier that tests a candidate presentation against
**5402 finite groups**. For each group $G$ it computes
$|\mathrm{Sur}(\text{presentation} \to G)| / |\mathrm{Aut}(G)|$ (surjections up
to automorphism) and checks it equals the ground-truth number of Galois
extensions of $\mathbb Q_2$ with Galois group $G$. A submission is correct iff
all 5402 counts match.

## Submission format

```
sigma,tau,x0,x1

[optional definitions: name = expression]

[relations: expressions that must equal the identity]
```

## Worked example for p = 3 (given; the analogue for p = 2 is what is sought)

```
sigma,tau,x0,x1

tau2 = tau^3011753745
sigma2 = sigma^3011753745
rho = tau2^4
angle = (x0*tau*x0^-1*tau)^2974571600
u = (x1*(rho^2)*x1*(rho^2))^2974571600
v = sigma2*tau2
w = (u*(v^2)*u^-1*(v^2))^2974571600
y1 = x1^rho*(u^v)*(w^(sigma2*tau2^2))*(w^(tau2^2))
comm = x1^-1*y1^-1*x1*y1

(x0^sigma)^-1*angle*x1^3*comm
```

This realizes NSW Theorem 7.5.14 for $p=3$: $\mathtt{tau2},\mathtt{sigma2}$ are
pro-$p$ projections, $\mathtt{angle}=\langle x_0,\tau\rangle$, $\mathtt{u},
\mathtt{w}$ are nested brackets, and the relation reads
$x_0^{\sigma}=\langle x_0,\tau\rangle\, x_1^{p}\,[x_1,y_1]$.

## Author's guidance

"I don't expect major conceptual developments to be required — the answer is
likely to be messy." So the sought relation may involve many intermediate
definitions (as in the $p=3$ example), not just a single short commutator word.

## Warm-up

The presentation for $\mathrm{Gal}(\overline{\mathbb Q}_3/\mathbb Q_3)$ is the
example above; the task is the genuinely open $p=2$ case.

The absolute Galois group of Q2
Problem statement
Give a presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers as a profinite group.
Overview
The absolute Galois group Gal(K/K) of a field K is the projective limit of all of the finite Galois groups Gal(E/K).
It packages together the information about all finite extensions, and studying the Galois group of the rational field
Q is a central problem in algebraic number theory. One method for approaching this group is to study the analogous
Galois groups of p-adic fields Qp. In this case, there is an explicit presentation of Gal(Qp/Qp) for p > 2, described
in Section . For p = 2, we have a description for the absolute Galois group of some extensions of Q2, but not for Q2
itself. Finding such a description would fill in a gap in our explicit understanding of Galois groups, and would have
consequences in terms of counting p-adic fields with a given Galois group.

Importance
Galois representations are at the core of many problems in modern number theory. There are many perspectives on
Galois representations, but if you have access to a presentation of an absolute Galois group then they can be viewed
very concretely. The case of p = 2 often needs to be handled separately in number theory, and global applications
often require the ability to handle all primes.
Di!culty
The last major work on finding presentations for p-adic fields was done in the 1980s. To a large extent, I believe that
the di!culty is that the answer is likely to be messy, as evidenced by the complicated relation needed in the case
that p is odd. Hopefully, this feature makes the problem a good target for solution by an AI since I don’t expect
major conceptional developments to be required.
Submitting a presentation
A submission for this problem consists of a file with the following form:
variables
definitions
relations
• Sections should be separated by single blank lines, and the definitions section is optional.
• The variables should consist of a comma separated list of variable names on one line corresponding to the
generators of the presentation. The first and second generators (say ϑ and ϖ respectively) are distinguished,
and must satisfy the implicit relation ϖε = ϖ p (which should not be included in the relations section). All
later generators should be contained within the p-core of the group (the intersection of all p-Sylow subgroups).
• In the definitions section, you may define additional variables in terms of the generators and variables
defined earlier within the definitions section. Each line should take the form var = expr, where expr is an
expression built using group operations.
• In the relations section, you should give the relations as expressions in terms of variables defined in the
previous two sections. If there are multiple relations, give one on each line.
Expressions in profinite groups may involve exponents lying in Zˆ. To specify such a constant, you should provide

exponents of all the groups being used to verify the presentation.

As before, the proof-level exponent is the genuine profinite idempotent \(\omega_2\in\widehat{\mathbb Z}\), not the literal integer. Turn 54 made the ramified Kummer/Shapiro step exact; the remaining local-Arf proof component worth isolating is the unramified case. 

Let \(F=\mathbb Q_2\), and let \(V\) be a nontrivial **unramified**, self-dual simple \(\mathbb F_2[G_F]\)-module with invariant nonsingular quadratic form \(q\). Let

\[
Q_F:H^1(F,V)\to H^2(F,\mathbb F_2)\simeq \mathbb F_2
\]

be the quadratic connecting map attached to the extraspecial extension defined by \(q\).

Choose the finite unramified splitting field \(K/F\), with cyclic odd Galois group \(H=\langle s\rangle\). Since \(|H|\) is odd,

\[
H^1(H,V)=H^2(H,V)=0,
\]

so Hochschild–Serre and Kummer give

\[
H^1(F,V)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_H(V^\vee,K^\times/K^{\times 2}).
\]

For an unramified \(K/F\),

\[
K^\times/K^{\times 2}\simeq \mathbb F_2\{\pi\}\oplus \mathbb F_2\{-1\}\oplus k_K
\]

as an \(\mathbb F_2[H]\)-module, up to the canonical filtration identification; the first two summands are trivial. Since \(V\) is nontrivial simple, only the \(k_K\)-summand contributes. Thus

\[
H^1(F,V)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}_H(V^\vee,k_K).
\]

Write

\[
D=\operatorname{End}_H(V)\simeq \mathbb F_{2^{2m}},\qquad D_0=\mathbb F_{2^m},
\]

where the involution induced by the self-duality is

\[
x\mapsto x^*=x^{2^m}.
\]

Then \(H^1(F,V)\) is one-dimensional over \(D\). The norm-one group

\[
U=\{u\in D^\times: uu^*=1\}
\]

acts on \(H^1(F,V)\) by \(D\)-multiplication, and it preserves \(Q_F\), because these are precisely coefficient-module automorphisms preserving the extraspecial extension.

There are no nonzero \(U\)-invariant linear forms. Indeed, identifying the dual of \(D\) with \(D\) by the absolute trace pairing, a fixed linear form would be represented by some \(y\in D\) satisfying

\[
\operatorname{Tr}_{D/\mathbb F_2}(yux)=\operatorname{Tr}_{D/\mathbb F_2}(yx)
\]

for every \(x\in D\) and every \(u\in U\). Hence \(y(u-1)=0\) for all \(u\), forcing \(y=0\).

Every \(U\)-invariant nonsingular quadratic form on this one-dimensional \(D\)-space is therefore

\[
Q_a(x)=\operatorname{Tr}_{D_0/\mathbb F_2}(a\,xx^*),\qquad a\in D_0^\times.
\]

The zero count is immediate from the norm map \(N:D^\times\to D_0^\times\). The fiber of each nonzero norm value has size \(2^m+1\), and the equation \(\operatorname{Tr}_{D_0/\mathbb F_2}(ay)=0\) has \(2^{m-1}-1\) nonzero solutions \(y\in D_0^\times\). Hence

\[
\#Q_a^{-1}(0)=1+(2^m+1)(2^{m-1}-1)
=2^{2m-1}-2^{m-1}.
\]

So \(Q_F\) is minus type:

\[
\operatorname{Arf}(Q_F)=1.
\]

This proves the unramified half of the local Arf theorem without relying on a symmetry slogan. Together with Turn 54’s ramified projectivity/deep-unit argument, the full local determinant theorem now reads:

\[
\operatorname{Arf}(Q_F)=
\begin{cases}
1,& V \text{ unramified},\\
0,& V \text{ ramified}.
\end{cases}
\]

This matches the candidate-side calculation already in the stack: for unramified \(V\), \(S_2=1\), so

\[
Q_{A_2}(c)=q(c)
\]

and the same norm-trace calculation gives Arf invariant \(1\); for ramified \(V\), the Dickson correction flips the candidate form to hyperbolic, matching the local result.
